title: Gynecomastia in an Adolescent Male: A Hypogonadotropic Pattern and a Suboptimal Testosterone Script
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: reading the low-T/low-LH pattern, why MRI sella is mandatory despite a normal prolactin, CDGP vs permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, and critiquing 'Testoviron 100 mg monthly' for puberty induction
authors: Endocrine residency training module — illustrative teaching case (OCR of clinical document)

pmh: Bone age 14 years (mildly delayed for chronological age 15)
Testicular volume 8 mL bilaterally (small for age 15)
medications: Testoviron Depot 100 mg (testosterone enanthate) IM once a month — regimen critiqued in items

caseDescription: A 15-year-old boy presents with painful bilateral gynecomastia. He has a mildly delayed bone age (14 years), a normal 46,XY karyotype, and bilateral testicular volumes of 8 mL (small for age). Hormones show a low-normal testosterone (0.339 ng/mL) with a low-normal LH (2.05) and normal FSH (4.14) — an inappropriately non-elevated gonadotropin response to a low testosterone, i.e. a hypogonadotropic pattern — and a normal prolactin (4.5). He was referred for an MRI sella/suprasella (pituitary protocol) and started on Testoviron Depot (testosterone enanthate) 100 mg IM once monthly. The teaching case asks residents to interpret the hypogonadotropic pattern, justify the MRI despite the normal prolactin, separate constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) from permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (HH), critique the testosterone regimen, and work up the gynecomastia itself.
keyFindings: Low-normal testosterone with low-normal LH and normal FSH = hypogonadotropic (not primary) pattern
Testicular volume 8 mL bilaterally — small for age 15, supports hypogonadism over simple pubertal gynecomastia
Normal prolactin does NOT exclude a structural sellar/hypothalamic lesion — MRI still indicated
Testoviron 100 mg monthly is pharmacokinetically suboptimal and leaves the patient hypogonadal most of the cycle
finalDiagnosis: Adolescent gynecomastia on a background of a hypogonadotropic (central) hypogonadism pattern in a 46,XY male — the central diagnostic split is constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) versus permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (including Kallmann syndrome). Mandatory steps before committing to therapy: MRI sella/suprasella to exclude a structural hypothalamic-pituitary lesion (despite the normal prolactin), and a tumour screen for the gynecomastia (scrotal ultrasound, β-hCG, estradiol, DHEAS). Inhibin B, AMH and a GnRH-stimulation test, plus 6-12 months of observation, help distinguish CDGP from HH. The prescribed Testoviron 100 mg monthly should be corrected to a low-dose induction regimen (e.g. testosterone enanthate 50 mg IM every 2 weeks).
learningObjectives: Recognise the low-testosterone/low-LH pattern as hypogonadotropic (central) rather than primary testicular failure.
Justify MRI sella/suprasella in central hypogonadism even when prolactin is normal.
Build a structured differential for adolescent gynecomastia and the tests that exclude tumour causes.
Distinguish CDGP from permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism using inhibin B, AMH, GnRH testing and observation.
Critique and correct an inappropriate testosterone induction regimen and know when treating the cause comes first.

items.title: How to read the low-testosterone / low-LH pattern as central hypogonadism
items.content: The single most important interpretive step here is to notice that the gonadotropins are inappropriately non-elevated. In primary hypogonadism (testicular failure) a low testosterone removes negative feedback, so LH and FSH rise — they are HIGH. In this boy testosterone is low-normal (0.339 ng/mL) yet LH is only low-normal (2.05) and FSH normal (4.14): the pituitary is not mounting the expected compensatory rise, which defines a hypogonadotropic (central, secondary) pattern pointing to the hypothalamus or pituitary. Combined with a testicular volume of 8 mL (small for 15) and a delayed bone age, this is the picture of either constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) or permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (HH). The classic error is to read 'normal' gonadotropins reassuringly without asking whether they are appropriate for the testosterone level.
keyPoints: Primary (testicular) failure → LH/FSH HIGH; central (HH/CDGP) → LH/FSH low or inappropriately normal.
Low-normal T with low-normal LH = hypogonadotropic pattern (hypothalamic/pituitary).
Interpret gonadotropins relative to the testosterone, not against the lab range alone.

items.title: Why MRI sella/suprasella is mandatory despite a normal prolactin
items.content: A normal prolactin (4.5) makes a prolactinoma unlikely but does not exclude structural hypothalamic-pituitary disease, so the MRI is correctly ordered. Once a central (hypogonadotropic) pattern is established in an adolescent, a pituitary-protocol MRI is needed to exclude lesions that present with isolated or evolving gonadotropin deficiency and a normal prolactin: craniopharyngioma, non-functioning pituitary adenoma, infiltrative disease (Langerhans cell histiocytosis, sarcoidosis), other hypothalamic mass lesions, and the structural olfactory-bulb/tract abnormalities of Kallmann syndrome. The pitfall to avoid is treating the low testosterone with replacement before imaging — if a tumour is present it must be addressed first. Hyperprolactinaemia is only one of several mechanisms; its absence never substitutes for imaging in central hypogonadism.
keyPoints: Normal prolactin lowers the chance of prolactinoma but does not exclude a structural lesion.
MRI rules out craniopharyngioma, adenoma, infiltrative disease, hypothalamic lesions, Kallmann structural defects.
Image before committing to testosterone — a tumour must be treated first.

items.title: How to frame the differential for gynecomastia in an adolescent
items.content: Gynecomastia reflects a relative excess of estrogen action over androgen action at the breast. Build the differential in tiers. Physiological pubertal gynecomastia (ages ~10-16, bilateral, often tender, self-limiting) is by far the commonest and the default in a mid-pubertal boy. Pathological causes must still be screened: hypogonadism (HH including Kallmann, or CDGP) as in this patient; pituitary disease (prolactinoma, other tumours); estrogen- or hCG-secreting tumours (testicular Sertoli/Leydig-cell tumours — often unilateral, adrenal tumours, germ-cell tumours); systemic disease (liver disease, thyroid dysfunction, obesity/increased aromatisation); genetic causes (Klinefelter 47,XXY — excluded here by karyotype); and drugs/substances (anabolic steroids, anti-androgens, cannabis, and many others). A careful drug and substance history is essential and frequently overlooked.
keyPoints: Mechanism = estrogen action exceeding androgen action at the breast.
Pubertal gynecomastia is commonest/self-limiting, but screen for hypogonadism, tumours (hCG/estrogen), systemic disease and drugs.
Karyotype 46,XY excludes Klinefelter; always take a drug/substance history.

items.title: Why separating CDGP from permanent HH is the central challenge at this age
items.content: Both CDGP and permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism present in adolescence with delayed puberty, delayed bone age and a hypogonadotropic biochemistry — and at a single time point they can be biochemically indistinguishable. The distinction is critical because CDGP is a normal variant that will progress spontaneously (these boys just start late, often with a positive family history of delayed puberty), whereas HH will not progress without treatment and signals a permanent deficit (sometimes a structural lesion or Kallmann syndrome). Testicular volume offers a clue — very small testes (<4 mL) lean toward HH, larger volumes toward incipient puberty/CDGP — but this patient's 8 mL is genuinely intermediate and does not settle it. Because no single test is definitive early, the honest position is that distinguishing the two often needs adjunctive tests plus longitudinal observation.
keyPoints: CDGP (a normal late-starting variant, often familial) WILL progress; HH will NOT without treatment.
They can be biochemically identical at one time point — testicular volume helps but 8 mL is intermediate.
Definitive distinction often requires adjunctive testing plus 6-12 months of observation.

items.title: How inhibin B, AMH and GnRH testing help separate CDGP from HH
items.content: When a single hormone panel cannot separate CDGP from HH, use markers of Sertoli-cell function and gonadotrope reserve. Inhibin B (a Sertoli-cell product reflecting the functioning tubular compartment) tends to be low in HH and preserved/normal in CDGP. AMH (also Sertoli-derived) similarly indicates Sertoli-cell function — a low AMH supports impaired Sertoli function and HH. A GnRH (or GnRH-agonist) stimulation test probes pituitary gonadotrope reserve: a brisk LH response suggests an intact axis poised to progress (CDGP), whereas a blunted response is more consistent with HH — though there is overlap and no test is perfect. The practical message: combine these adjuncts with clinical course rather than relying on any one result, and re-evaluate over time.
keyPoints: Inhibin B: low in HH, preserved in CDGP (Sertoli-cell/tubular marker).
Low AMH supports poor Sertoli-cell function → HH; GnRH/GnRHa test gauges gonadotrope reserve (brisk LH → CDGP).
All have overlap — interpret together with longitudinal follow-up.

items.title: Why 'Testoviron 100 mg monthly' is pharmacokinetically wrong
items.content: Testosterone enanthate has a half-life of roughly 4.5 days, so a 100 mg depot dose given only once every 30 days produces a brief peak in the first 2-3 days and then falls toward near-castrate levels for the remaining ~3 weeks of the cycle. The patient therefore spends most of each month hypogonadal — with swings in mood, energy and libido and no steady physiological exposure. For pubertal induction the goal is a low, gradually escalating, steadier level, not a monthly spike-and-trough. The prescribed regimen mismatches the ester's kinetics to the dosing interval and undertreats. (The only testosterone designed for ~10-14-weekly dosing is long-acting testosterone undecanoate, which is generally not the first choice for adolescent puberty induction.)
keyPoints: Enanthate t½ ≈ 4.5 days: monthly dosing peaks early then drops to near-castrate for ~3 weeks.
Monthly Testoviron leaves the boy hypogonadal most of the cycle — undertreatment with spike-and-trough.
Only undecanoate is dosed at ~10-14-week intervals, and it is not first-line for adolescent induction.

items.title: How to dose testosterone correctly (induction vs replacement)
items.content: Match the regimen to the goal. For gradual, physiological pubertal induction, start low and escalate slowly — e.g. testosterone enanthate ~50 mg IM every 2 weeks, increasing the dose stepwise roughly every 6 months toward adult replacement, which mimics the natural pubertal rise and protects predicted height by not advancing bone age too fast. For established adult replacement, testosterone enanthate/cypionate 100-200 mg IM every 2 weeks (or other approved formulations) is standard; long-acting undecanoate 1000 mg every 10-14 weeks is an adult option. Two caveats specific to this case: exclude a structural CNS lesion first (await MRI), and if CDGP is the working diagnosis a short 3-6 month low-dose testosterone trial can be used both to 'kick-start' and as a diagnostic aid — after which spontaneous puberty should continue in CDGP but not in permanent HH.
keyPoints: Induction: testosterone enanthate ~50 mg IM every 2 weeks, escalate ~6-monthly.
Adult replacement: enanthate/cypionate 100-200 mg every 2 weeks, or undecanoate every 10-14 weeks.
Exclude a CNS lesion first; in suspected CDGP a 3-6 month low-dose trial is both therapeutic and diagnostic.

items.title: Why you must treat the cause before reaching for testosterone
items.content: Starting testosterone before completing the workup can mask or delay treatment of a serious underlying cause and confound the diagnostic picture. The sequence: complete the central-hypogonadism evaluation, get the MRI, and screen the gynecomastia for tumour causes — then decide. If the MRI shows a craniopharyngioma or other lesion, that is treated first (surgery, and for a prolactinoma a dopamine agonist such as cabergoline). If an estrogen/hCG-secreting tumour is found, it is removed/treated. Only once structural and tumour causes are addressed (or excluded) is testosterone replacement appropriate — and even then the choice between a short CDGP trial and long-term HH replacement depends on the evolving diagnosis. Premature replacement also suppresses the very gonadotropins you may need to interpret.
keyPoints: Finish the workup (MRI + tumour screen) before committing to testosterone.
Treat structural/tumour causes first (craniopharyngioma → surgery; prolactinoma → cabergoline; estrogen/hCG tumour → resect).
Premature testosterone masks the cause and suppresses gonadotropins needed for diagnosis.

items.title: How to decide when adolescent gynecomastia needs treatment
items.content: Most pubertal gynecomastia regresses within months to ~2 years and needs only reassurance and observation, plus correction of any underlying hypogonadism/cause. Intervene when it has persisted beyond ~2 years (by which point fibrosis makes spontaneous and medical resolution unlikely) or is causing significant physical or psychological distress. Medical therapy works best early, before fibrosis sets in: a selective estrogen-receptor modulator — tamoxifen 10-20 mg/day (or raloxifene 60 mg/day) for 3-6 months — can reduce tender glandular tissue. Long-standing, fibrotic or cosmetically distressing gynecomastia is treated surgically (subcutaneous mastectomy). Crucially, treating the gynecomastia is separate from, and does not substitute for, diagnosing and treating the underlying hypogonadism.
keyPoints: Reassure/observe if recent (<2 years) and not distressing — most pubertal gynecomastia regresses.
Treat if >2 years or significant distress; SERMs (tamoxifen/raloxifene) work best before fibrosis.
Fibrotic/long-standing disease → surgery; manage gynecomastia separately from the underlying cause.

items.title: How to complete the workup — the tests not yet done
items.content: Several high-yield steps were missing from the initial plan. Always ask about the sense of smell — anosmia/hyposmia points to Kallmann syndrome (HH with olfactory-bulb agenesis). Document height, weight, BMI and growth velocity, and consider a DXA because chronic hypogonadism harms bone. Targeted tumour/cause screen: estradiol (high → testicular/adrenal estrogen source), β-hCG (high → germ-cell tumour), DHEAS/androstenedione (adrenal cause), TSH and free T4 (hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism can cause gynecomastia), and liver function tests (altered estrogen clearance). A scrotal ultrasound confirms testicular volume and excludes a testicular tumour. Inhibin B and AMH help separate CDGP from HH. The MRI pituitary is already appropriately ordered. Together these define both the cause of the central hypogonadism and the cause of the gynecomastia.
keyPoints: Ask about smell (Kallmann); record growth/BMI; DXA for bone effects of hypogonadism.
Cause screen: estradiol, β-hCG, DHEAS/androstenedione, TFTs, LFTs, scrotal ultrasound.
Inhibin B + AMH for CDGP-vs-HH; MRI pituitary already ordered.

items.question: Why does this boy's biochemistry indicate central (hypogonadotropic) rather than primary hypogonadism?
items.answer: Because the gonadotropins are inappropriately non-elevated for the low testosterone. In primary testicular failure, low testosterone releases negative feedback and LH/FSH rise (become high). Here testosterone is low-normal while LH is low-normal and FSH normal — no compensatory rise — which localises the problem to the hypothalamus or pituitary (a hypogonadotropic pattern), with CDGP and permanent HH as the main possibilities.

items.question: How would you correct the prescribed 'Testoviron 100 mg monthly' for this adolescent?
items.answer: First withhold definitive therapy until the MRI and tumour screen are complete. For pubertal induction, replace the monthly regimen with low-dose testosterone enanthate (~50 mg IM every 2 weeks), escalating roughly every 6 months toward adult replacement — this gives steadier, more physiological levels than a once-monthly spike-and-trough (enanthate t½ ≈ 4.5 days). If CDGP is suspected, a 3-6 month low-dose course doubles as a diagnostic trial; if permanent HH is confirmed, continue long-term replacement and plan fertility (gonadotropin therapy) later.

items.question: Prolactin is normal at 4.5 ng/mL. Why is an MRI sella/suprasella still indicated?
items.answer: Because a normal prolactin only makes a prolactinoma unlikely; it does not exclude other structural hypothalamic-pituitary disease. In an adolescent with a confirmed hypogonadotropic pattern, MRI is needed to exclude craniopharyngioma, non-functioning pituitary adenoma, infiltrative disease (e.g. Langerhans cell histiocytosis, sarcoidosis), other hypothalamic lesions, and the structural olfactory defects of Kallmann syndrome — all of which can present with central hypogonadism and a normal prolactin.

items.question: Which tests help distinguish constitutional delay (CDGP) from permanent hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (HH), and what pattern favours HH?
items.answer: Inhibin B and AMH (Sertoli-cell markers) and a GnRH/GnRH-agonist stimulation test, alongside testicular volume and longitudinal observation. Low inhibin B, low AMH and a blunted LH response to GnRH favour permanent HH, whereas preserved inhibin B/AMH and a brisk LH response favour CDGP. Because of overlap, definitive distinction often requires 6-12 months of follow-up to see whether puberty progresses spontaneously.

items.stem: A 15-year-old boy with gynecomastia has testosterone 0.339 ng/mL (low-normal), LH 2.05 (low-normal) and FSH 4.14 (normal). How is this pattern best classified?
options: Primary hypogonadism (testicular failure)
Hypogonadotropic (central) hypogonadism pattern
Androgen insensitivity syndrome
Normal mid-pubertal hormones requiring no further thought
items.explanation: A low testosterone with gonadotropins that are not elevated (low-normal LH, normal FSH) indicates failure of the expected compensatory rise — a hypogonadotropic (central) pattern localising to the hypothalamus/pituitary. Primary failure would show high LH/FSH. The differential is then CDGP vs permanent HH.

items.stem: The prolactin is normal. What is the correct stance on the ordered MRI sella/suprasella?
options: Cancel it — a normal prolactin excludes pituitary pathology
Proceed — a normal prolactin does not exclude a structural hypothalamic-pituitary lesion in central hypogonadism
Replace it with a plain CT head
Defer it until after 6 months of testosterone therapy
items.explanation: A normal prolactin only makes a prolactinoma unlikely. In a confirmed hypogonadotropic pattern, MRI is mandatory to exclude craniopharyngioma, adenoma, infiltrative/hypothalamic disease and Kallmann-type structural defects — and imaging should precede testosterone therapy so any lesion is treated first.

items.stem: Why is testosterone enanthate 100 mg IM once monthly an inappropriate regimen here?
options: Enanthate cannot be given intramuscularly
Its ~4.5-day half-life means monthly dosing leaves the patient hypogonadal for most of the cycle
100 mg is an overdose that will cause polycythaemia within days
Monthly dosing advances bone age too slowly to ever induce puberty
items.explanation: Testosterone enanthate has a half-life of about 4.5 days, so a once-monthly 100 mg dose peaks briefly then falls to near-castrate levels for roughly three weeks — the boy is hypogonadal most of the month. Physiological induction uses low-dose enanthate every ~2 weeks; only long-acting undecanoate is dosed at 10-14-week intervals.

items.stem: For gradual pubertal induction in this adolescent (after a CNS lesion has been excluded), which regimen is most appropriate?
options: Testosterone enanthate 100 mg IM monthly
Testosterone enanthate ~50 mg IM every 2 weeks, escalating roughly every 6 months
Testosterone undecanoate 1000 mg every 12 weeks
Topical high-dose testosterone gel to rapidly normalise levels
items.explanation: Pubertal induction starts low and escalates slowly to mimic physiology and protect predicted height — typically testosterone enanthate ~50 mg every 2 weeks, increased stepwise about every 6 months. Monthly enanthate gives spike-and-trough kinetics; undecanoate is an adult long-acting option, not first-line for adolescent induction; and rapid high-dose normalisation is not the goal.

items.stem: Which finding in this case most directly excludes Klinefelter syndrome as the cause of the gynecomastia?
options: Normal prolactin
Bone age of 14 years
Karyotype 46,XY
Testicular volume of 8 mL
items.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome is defined by a 47,XXY (or variant) karyotype. A normal 46,XY karyotype excludes it. Prolactin, bone age and testicular volume do not address the chromosomal diagnosis (and Klinefelter classically causes small firm testes with high LH/FSH — a primary pattern).

items.stem: His gynecomastia has been present for about 8 months and is mildly tender but not distressing. After addressing the hypogonadism workup, what is the most appropriate approach to the breast tissue itself?
options: Immediate subcutaneous mastectomy
Reassurance and observation, as most pubertal gynecomastia of short duration regresses
Start tamoxifen indefinitely
High-dose testosterone to shrink the breast tissue quickly
items.explanation: Pubertal gynecomastia of short duration (<2 years) that is not causing significant distress is managed with reassurance and observation, while the underlying hypogonadism is worked up. A SERM (tamoxifen/raloxifene, 3-6 months) is considered if it persists or distresses; surgery is reserved for long-standing, fibrotic or distressing disease.

items.statement: A normal serum prolactin reliably excludes a structural pituitary or hypothalamic lesion in an adolescent with central hypogonadism.
items.explanation: False. A normal prolactin only makes a prolactinoma less likely. Craniopharyngioma, non-functioning adenoma, infiltrative disease and hypothalamic lesions can all cause central hypogonadism with a normal prolactin, so MRI sella/suprasella is still required.

items.statement: Asking about the sense of smell is an important part of evaluating this boy's hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
items.explanation: True. Anosmia or hyposmia points to Kallmann syndrome — hypogonadotropic hypogonadism due to defective GnRH-neuron migration with olfactory-bulb agenesis. Screening the sense of smell is a simple, high-yield step that is frequently omitted.

items.assertion: Testosterone replacement should not be started in this boy until the MRI sella/suprasella result is available.
items.reason: If a structural lesion such as a craniopharyngioma is present, the underlying cause must be treated first, and premature testosterone can mask the picture and suppress gonadotropins.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: a central hypogonadism pattern mandates excluding a structural lesion before committing to replacement, because a tumour needs treating first and starting testosterone prematurely confounds the evaluation (and suppresses the gonadotropins needed to interpret the axis). Hence option 0.

items.assertion: This patient's normal-range LH and FSH can be interpreted as a reassuring, normal result.
items.reason: Gonadotropin levels should always be interpreted against the laboratory reference range rather than against the prevailing testosterone level.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. LH/FSH that are merely 'in range' are inappropriate when testosterone is low — the expected response to low testosterone is a rise in gonadotropins, so 'normal' values actually indicate a hypogonadotropic problem. Gonadotropins must be interpreted relative to the testosterone level, not the reference range alone. Both false = option 4.
